comments:
Summary:
This manuscript presents ORIGAMI, an ASIC accelerator for convolutional neural networks (ConvNets) focused on dramatically reducing external memory bandwidth while achieving high throughput and good area/energy efficiency. The authors describe an architecture that reuses input image patches and filter data to lower I/O, a fixed-point datapath (12-bit), a layout implemented in UMC 65 nm (3.09 mm^2 core), and report peak/core-throughputs (274 GOp/s peak, 203 GOp/s effective), energy figures (369 GOp/s/W excluding pads, 220 GOp/s/W including pads), and a low external I/O requirement (525 MB/s full-duplex). The paper compares the design to prior FPGA/ASIC works such as neuFlow and nn-X and discusses projected scaling to 28 nm.

Strengths:
- Addresses a concrete and important bottleneck for practical ConvNet accelerators: external memory bandwidth and I/O pin limits.
- Presents a concrete architecture and implementation, including fixed-point precision study, area breakdown and throughput/efficiency analysis.
- Achieves competitive area efficiency (GOp/s/mm^2) and low I/O per operation numbers relative to cited prior work.
- Includes implementation details (clocking approach, SRAM partitioning, test structures) that increase confidence in the engineering effort.

Weaknesses and concerns:
- Measurement provenance is unclear. The manuscript states the chip was sent for tapeout and that power usage was "simulated running with real data"; it is not explicit whether the reported power/throughput numbers are from silicon measurements or pre-silicon simulation. This distinction is crucial for assessing the results. The reviewer could only rely on the provided text, which appears to indicate mostly simulation-based power results.
- System-level energy efficiency relies heavily on projected external memory energy (scaled to LPDDR3 at 28 nm) rather than measured system-level power. These projections are useful but should be clearly separated from measured data and validated when silicon/board measurements are available.
- The fixed-point accuracy analysis uses a relatively small dataset (Stanford backgrounds) with 150 test images; reporting a <0.5% drop in per-pixel accuracy is encouraging but limited. The generality of 12-bit choices across a broader set of networks (varying depth, normalization layers, modern activations) is not demonstrated.
- The architecture appears to fix the filter size (k chosen and larger filters must be decomposed or padded). This imposes constraints and potential performance penalties for networks with varied filter shapes; the practical impact should be quantified.
- Image height is limited to 512 pixels by on-chip SRAM, requiring tiling and overlap for larger images; the resulting memory and bandwidth overheads for tiling are not experimentally detailed.
- Some efficiency losses occur when channel counts do not align with the processing block size (e.g., stage 1 with 3 input channels); the manuscript acknowledges this but lacks comprehensive mitigation strategies.
- The provided manuscript text contains repeated / duplicated passages and some missing numerics (likely OCR/artifact in the provided copy). While this may be due to the text source supplied to the reviewer, it makes parts of the exposition harder to follow. The final submission should be carefully proofread.

Suggestions for improvement / future work:
- Clarify whether the reported throughput and power are measured silicon results or simulation; if simulation, report testchip measurement results when available and update comparisons.
- Provide end-to-end system-level measurements including off-chip memory (DRAM) energy and the package/pin drivers when possible; these are critical to validate the low I/O benefit claimed.
- Evaluate the accelerator on a broader set of modern ConvNets (different depths, diverse filter sizes, batch/stride variants) and report accuracy and performance, to demonstrate generality of the 12-bit choice and the filter-size handling strategy.
- Discuss programmability and flexibility more explicitly: ability to support varying filter sizes efficiently, handling of non-convolution operations (pooling, activations) and the overhead of off-chip summation when partitioning channels.
- Provide more detail on latency and throughput under realistic workloads (e.g., full-HD images, multi-stage pipelining) and quantification of tiling overheads.
- Release (or offer) more implementation details / simulation scripts to improve reproducibility.

Overall assessment:
The paper presents a meaningful hardware contribution focused on reducing the memory bandwidth bottleneck for ConvNet accelerators, with solid architecture decisions and an implementation in 65 nm technology. The reported area and arithmetic-efficiency numbers are promising and the focus on I/O-per-operation is an important and under-addressed metric. However, the reliance on simulation/projections for some system-level numbers, limited fixed-point evaluation dataset, and restrictions (fixed filter size, image height limit) temper the strength of the claims. With clearer presentation of measurement provenance, broader evaluation, and measured system-level power including DRAM, this work would be a strong contribution to the community.
